threats.title: Complete Governance Bypass via Execution of Defeated Proposals
threats.hypothesis_description: The execute function in the Governor contract allows any proposal that has reached the Defeated or Expired state to be executed, completely bypassing the voting consensus mechanism.
threats.investigation_rationale: A critical logic error in the state validation allows an attacker to execute arbitrary malicious payloads by simply proposing them and waiting for the voting period to end, regardless of the vote outcome.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(status == ProposalState.Succeeded || status == ProposalState.Defeated || status == ProposalState.Expired)

preconditions: Attacker has enough voting power to meet proposalThreshold (which is 0 by default)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Execution of proposals is permitted for failure states (Defeated, Expired)

operational_questions: Can an attacker execute a proposal that was unanimously voted against?
Are there any secondary checks preventing execution of defeated proposals?
validation_requirements: Create a proposal, vote against it, wait for deadline, and attempt execution
Verify if arbitrary contract calls (targets/calldatas) succeed

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Submit a malicious governance proposal (e.g., transferring treasury funds)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Wait for the voting period to conclude (proposal becomes Defeated or Expired)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call execute() on the Governor contract with the proposal parameters
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: The contract executes the malicious payload because the require statement allows Defeated/Expired states

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Full protocol takeover via governance execution
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete loss of protocol funds and administrative control
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Extreme - attacker can use governance to block upgrades or recovery attempts

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Requires waiting for voting delay and period

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Execution of proposals with Defeated or Expired status
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Malicious proposals being submitted despite obvious community rejection
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Execution called immediately after voting period ends for failed proposals

threats.title: Arbitrary Token Whitelisting and Gauge Creation via Unvalidated Genesis Pool
threats.hypothesis_description: The depositToken function in GenesisPoolManager does not verify that the provided genesisPool address was created by the protocol's factory. An attacker can pass a malicious contract that returns true, allowing them to whitelist arbitrary tokens and create gauges for arbitrary pairs.
threats.investigation_rationale: Lack of origin validation on the genesisPool parameter allows an attacker to spoof a pool, returning true for eligibility and tricking the manager into deploying a gauge for a malicious or unauthorized pair, and whitelisting arbitrary tokens.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Calls IGenesisPool(genesisPool).depositToken without verifying genesisPool against genesisFactory

preconditions: Attacker can deploy a smart contract

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: User-provided contract address is trusted for critical state transitions (whitelisting, gauge creation)

operational_questions: Can the attacker successfully create a gauge for a fake pair?
Does whitelisting an arbitrary token impact other protocol mechanics?
validation_requirements: Test depositToken with a mock IGenesisPool contract
Verify if tokenHandler.whitelistToken accepts the spoofed token

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Deploy a mock IGenesisPool contract that returns true for depositToken and provides attacker-controlled addresses for getGenesisInfo and getLiquidityPoolInfo
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call GenesisPoolManager.depositToken with the mock contract address
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Manager calls tokenHandler.whitelistToken with the attacker's token
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Manager calls gaugeManager.createGauge with the attacker's pair address

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Bypasses governance whitelisting and gauge creation controls
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Pollutes tokenHandler and gaugeManager with fake entries, potentially diverting emissions
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires governance to manually remove fake gauges and blacklist tokens
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Token whitelisting, Gauge creation, Emissions distribution

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Calls to depositToken with unknown genesisPool addresses
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Unexpected tokens whitelisted or gauges created
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Rapid gauge creation for unapproved pairs

threats.title: Permanent Denial of Service for Router Updates via Inverted Access Check
threats.hypothesis_description: The setRouter function in GenesisPoolManager contains an inverted require statement that only allows setting the router to the zero address, permanently bricking the ability to update it to a valid address.
threats.investigation_rationale: A typo in the validation logic (== instead of !=) prevents the owner from setting a valid router address. If the router is ever set to address(0), all future pool launches will revert.
preconditions: Owner attempts to update the router address

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Admin function requires the new parameter to be the zero address
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time an update is attempted

operational_questions: Can the router be updated to a valid address through any other mechanism?
What happens to genesis pools if the router is set to address(0)?
validation_requirements: Test calling setRouter with a non-zero address (should revert)
Test calling setRouter with address(0) (should succeed)

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Owner realizes the current router needs to be updated (e.g., due to a DEX upgrade)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Owner calls setRouter with the new valid address
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Transaction reverts due to the inverted require statement
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: If owner mistakenly calls setRouter(address(0)), the router is permanently bricked

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Loss of administrative capability to update critical infrastructure
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: If router is set to zero, all future genesis pool launches will fail
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires a contract upgrade to fix the typo

threats.title: Retroactive Quorum Manipulation via Current Supply Usage
threats.hypothesis_description: The quorum calculation uses the current total supply of smNFTs instead of the historical supply at the proposal snapshot, allowing attackers to manipulate the quorum threshold during an active vote.
threats.investigation_rationale: The quorum function calls getsmNFTPastTotalSupply() without passing the blockTimestamp. This breaks the invariant that quorum requirements should be fixed at proposal creation, enabling retroactive manipulation.
preconditions: Ability to mint or burn smNFTs during an active voting period

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Quorum calculation ignores the provided blockTimestamp parameter

operational_questions: Can the total supply of smNFTs be changed significantly during a voting period?
Does the token contract support getsmNFTPastTotalSupply with a timestamp?
validation_requirements: Create a proposal, check initial quorum
Mint new smNFTs, check if quorum increases for the existing proposal

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker observes a proposal they want to defeat that is barely meeting quorum
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker mints a large number of smNFTs (if possible) or triggers actions that increase total supply
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: The quorum threshold retroactively increases for the active proposal
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: The proposal fails to meet the new, higher quorum and expires

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Governance process can be manipulated to block legitimate proposals
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Inability to pass necessary governance actions
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium - requires fixing the quorum calculation via upgrade

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: Sufficient capital to noticeably impact total supply
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Must be executed before the voting period ends

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Large changes in smNFT total supply during critical votes
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Proposals failing quorum unexpectedly at the last minute

threats.title: Bypass of Genesis Deposit Blackout Window
threats.hypothesis_description: The GenesisPool depositToken function fails to enforce the NO_GENESIS_DEPOSIT_WINDOW, allowing users to deposit right up until the epoch flip if the manager's check function isn't called exactly on time.
threats.investigation_rationale: The contract relies entirely on an external operational call (checkBeforeEpochFlip) to disable deposits. If this call is delayed, the intended deposit blackout window is bypassed, allowing late depositors an unfair advantage.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Checks block.timestamp >= genesisInfo.startTime but lacks check for end of deposit window

preconditions: checkBeforeEpochFlip is not called exactly 3 hours before the epoch ends

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Critical timing constraint is not enforced at the contract level

operational_questions: How reliable is the epochController in calling checkBeforeEpochFlip?
Can a user front-run the checkBeforeEpochFlip transaction to squeeze in a late deposit?
validation_requirements: Test depositing during the blackout window before checkBeforeEpochFlip is called

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker monitors the genesis pool as the epoch nears its end
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker waits for the NO_GENESIS_DEPOSIT_WINDOW to begin
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: If the keeper is delayed, attacker deposits tokens, gaining an informational advantage over users who respected the window
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker secures an allocation that should have been locked

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Unfair allocation distribution, violating protocol rules
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low - but damage is irreversible once launch occurs

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Deposits occurring within the blackout window
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Users consistently depositing right before the status change

threats.title: Permanent Loss of Incentive Tokens on Pool Disqualification
threats.hypothesis_description: If a pool is disqualified after transferring incentives to the external bribe, the token owner cannot claim their remaining incentives because the mapping is not zeroed out, causing the claim function to revert.
threats.investigation_rationale: transferIncentives moves tokens but leaves the incentives mapping intact. If the pool later becomes NOT_QUALIFIED, claimIncentives will attempt to transfer the full original amount, reverting due to insufficient balance.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Transfers tokens to external_bribe but does not set incentives[token] = 0

preconditions: Pool is subsequently set to NOT_QUALIFIED by the manager

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: State mapping is not updated after tokens are transferred out
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires manager to change status to NOT_QUALIFIED

operational_questions: Can a pool be disqualified after reaching PRE_LAUNCH?
Are there any other mechanisms for the token owner to recover incentives?
validation_requirements: Simulate a pool reaching PRE_LAUNCH, then being set to NOT_QUALIFIED
Attempt to call claimIncentives and verify it reverts

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Token owner adds incentives to a genesis pool
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Pool reaches PRE_LAUNCH, and transferIncentives sends tokens to the bribe contract
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Manager sets pool status to NOT_QUALIFIED (e.g., due to an operational issue)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Token owner calls claimIncentives to recover funds, but it reverts because the contract balance is 0 while the mapping still expects the full amount

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Token owner permanently loses access to any remaining claimable incentives
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires manual intervention or contract upgrade

threats.title: Genesis Pool Bricking via Zero Address Auction Fallback
threats.hypothesis_description: If the default auction at index 0 is removed, depositing a native token with an invalid auction index will silently set the pool's auction to the zero address, bricking all subsequent user deposits.
threats.investigation_rationale: The fallback logic assumes auctions(0) is always valid. If it's not, the pool is initialized with a zero address auction, causing getNativeTokenAmount calls to revert during user deposits.
deployment_assumptions: Admin removes the auction at index 0
threats.config_evidence: AuctionFactory.sol allows removing auctions via removeAuction()
preconditions: Auction at index 0 has been removed
Token owner provides an invalid auctionIndex

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Fallback logic does not validate the final resolved address
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires admin to have removed the default auction

operational_questions: Is it common operational practice to remove the default auction?
Can the auction address be updated after pool creation?
validation_requirements: Create a pool with an invalid index
Attempt to deposit tokens and observe revert

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Admin removes the outdated auction at index 0
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: User creates a genesis pool and provides an invalid auctionIndex
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: The manager falls back to index 0, which is now address(0), and sets it as the pool's auction
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: All users attempting to deposit tokens experience reverts because the pool tries to call getNativeTokenAmount on address(0)

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Specific genesis pools become completely unusable
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium - requires governance to call setAuction to fix it

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Pools created with auction set to address(0)
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Consistent reverts on depositToken for specific pools

threats.title: System-Wide Epoch Flip Denial of Service via Reverting Genesis Pool
threats.hypothesis_description: The epoch flip functions iterate over all live native tokens and make external calls without try-catch blocks. A single reverting pool can block the entire epoch transition for all pools.
threats.investigation_rationale: If any pool fails during checkAtEpochFlip or checkBeforeEpochFlip (e.g., due to a fee-on-transfer token or malicious pair), the entire transaction reverts, halting the lifecycle of all other pending pools.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Loops over liveNativeTokens and makes external state-changing calls without try-catch

preconditions: A genesis pool is approved that will revert during launch or disqualification (e.g., due to token mechanics or pair issues)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Unbounded loop making external calls without fault tolerance
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires governance to approve the problematic pool

operational_questions: Can governance easily remove a broken pool from liveNativeTokens?
validation_requirements: Deploy a pool with a token that reverts on transfer
Attempt to call checkBeforeEpochFlip and observe if it blocks other pools

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker proposes a genesis pool with a specially crafted token that behaves normally until epoch flip, then reverts on transfers
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Governance approves the pool, adding it to liveNativeTokens
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: At epoch flip, checkBeforeEpochFlip or checkAtEpochFlip attempts to process the pool and reverts
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: The epoch transition is blocked for all pools, causing a system-wide DoS
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Social engineering to get governance approval

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All genesis pools are stuck, unable to launch or disqualify
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires governance to reject the specific pool or upgrade the manager

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Failed calls to checkAtEpochFlip or checkBeforeEpochFlip

threats.title: Permanent Lock of Token Owner LP Tokens via Rounding Underflow
threats.hypothesis_description: The deductAmount function can underflow when calculating the token owner's deduction due to integer division rounding, permanently locking their LP tokens in the gauge.
threats.investigation_rationale: The calculation `userAmount -= (totalDeposits * pendingOwnerStaked) / _depositerLiquidity` can result in a value slightly larger than `userDeposits[account]`, causing a revert on the subsequent subtraction.

proof_of_signal.pattern: userAmount -= (totalDeposits * pendingOwnerStaked) / _depositerLiquidity; followed by userDeposits[account] -= userAmount;

preconditions: Token owner is also a depositor
Specific liquidity and deposit amounts that trigger unfavorable rounding

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Complex proportional math with multiple divisions applied to the same variable before subtraction

operational_questions: What exact values trigger this underflow?
Can the token owner bypass this by unstaking a different amount?
validation_requirements: Fuzz test deductAmount with various liquidity and deposit combinations to find underflow triggers

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Token owner deposits funding tokens into their own genesis pool
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Pool launches, and LP tokens are staked in the gauge
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Token owner attempts to unstake a specific amount of LP tokens
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: The deductAmount callback calculates a userAmount that exceeds their userDeposits balance due to rounding, causing an underflow revert

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Token owner cannot unstake their LP tokens
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires unstaking a different amount or contract upgrade

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Failed unstake transactions from token owners
